input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **cyber-physical infrastructure asset** within the **Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain**, specifically targeting **buildings** and their associated systems. The system encompasses **static models** (e.g., architectural, structural, or MEP designs) and **runtime data** (e.g., sensor feeds, operational logs, environmental metrics) to create a **virtual representation** of the physical asset. Key characteristics include:
  - **Heterogeneous and complex environments**: The system operates in **fragmented, multi-domain infrastructure ecosystems**, integrating disparate data sources (e.g., design models, IoT sensors, maintenance records) across the **building lifecycle** (design, construction, operation, refurbishment).
  - **Real-time data integration**: The physical twin relies on **dynamic data assimilation** from operational systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting, occupancy sensors) to mirror real-world conditions in the virtual domain.
  - **Lifecycle coverage**: The system is designed to support **end-to-end digital continuity**, from **initial design** through **construction phases** to **long-term operational optimization** and **adaptive refurbishment**.
  - **Stakeholder collaboration**: The twin facilitates **multi-disciplinary collaboration** among architects, engineers, facility managers, and operators, enabling **data-driven decision-making** across siloed domains.
  - **Predictive and adaptive capabilities**: The system is positioned to enable **predictive analytics** (e.g., failure forecasting, energy optimization) and **adaptive control** (e.g., dynamic system reconfiguration) via real-time insights.
  The suitability for digital twinning stems from its **highly interconnected, data-intensive, and lifecycle-dependent** nature, where virtual representations can unlock **operational efficiency**, **cost reduction**, and **resilience improvements** through simulation and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **domain-specific services** to users and the physical system in the AECO context:
  - **Real-time Monitoring**:
    - **Service Type**: Continuous **data ingestion and validation** from **IoT sensors**, **building management systems (BMS)**, and **operational logs** to create a **live virtual replica** of the physical asset.
    - **Target Users**: Facility managers, operations teams, and maintenance personnel.
    - **Capabilities**: Anomaly detection (e.g., equipment failures, energy inefficiencies), **performance benchmarking** against design specifications, and **compliance tracking** (e.g., regulatory or sustainability metrics).
  - **Predictive Analytics**:
    - **Service Type**: **Machine learning-driven forecasting** (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy demand prediction, occupancy patterns) using **historical and real-time data**.
    - **Target Users**: Strategic planners, energy managers, and asset owners.
    - **Capabilities**: **Failure probability modeling**, **lifecycle cost optimization**, and **resource allocation** (e.g., maintenance scheduling, energy procurement).
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Service Type**: **Multi-objective optimization** (e.g., energy efficiency, occupant comfort, structural integrity) via **simulation-based what-if analysis** and **automated control adjustments**.
    - **Target Users**: Design teams, energy consultants, and digital twins engineers.
    - **Capabilities**: **Dynamic system reconfiguration** (e.g., HVAC setpoints, lighting schedules), **cost-benefit tradeoff analysis**, and **adaptive refurbishment planning**.
  - **Control and Automation**:
    - **Service Type**: **Closed-loop control integration** with **physical systems** (e.g., BMS, IoT actuators) to execute **automated adjustments** based on DT insights.
    - **Target Users**: Automation engineers, facility operators.
    - **Capabilities**: **Real-time corrective actions** (e.g., fault mitigation, energy rerouting), **prescriptive control policies**, and **interoperability** with existing automation frameworks.
  - **Visualization and Decision Support**:
    - **Service Type**: **Immersive 3D/4D/5D modeling** (spatial + temporal + cost dimensions) with **interactive dashboards** and **augmented reality (AR)/virtual reality (VR) interfaces**.
    - **Target Users**: Architects, stakeholders, non-technical decision-makers.
    - **Capabilities**: **Scenario visualization** (e.g., refurbishment impact, failure propagation), **collaborative annotation**, and **decision support** via **simulation-driven insights**.
  - **Collaboration and Knowledge Management**:
    - **Service Type**: **Centralized data repository** with **version control**, **access permissions**, and **domain-specific ontologies** to unify fragmented AECO data.
    - **Target Users**: Cross-disciplinary teams (e.g., designers, contractors, operators).
    - **Capabilities**: **Conflict resolution** (e.g., design clashes), **knowledge reuse** across projects, and **standardized data exchange** (e.g., IFC, CityGML formats).
  The services are **data-driven**, **actionable**, and **lifecycle-aware**, addressing gaps in **fragmented AECO workflows** through **end-to-end digital continuity**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the **Digital Twin** in the AECO domain relies on the following **technical enablers and tooling**, as inferred from the context of **data-driven infrastructure management**:
  - **Integration Frameworks**:
    - **Role**: **Unified data and model integration** across heterogeneous sources (e.g., **BIM models**, **IoT streams**, **legacy systems**).
    - **Technologies**:
      - **Industry Foundation Classes (IFC)**: Standardized **building information modeling (BIM) data schema** for **semantic interoperability** between design and operational data.
      - **CityGML**: **Geospatial data model** for **urban infrastructure** and **multi-scale representations** (e.g., buildings, districts).
      - **Ontology-based mappings**: **Domain-specific ontologies** (e.g., **IFCO**, **DOLCE**) to resolve **semantic heterogeneity** in AECO data.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Role**: **Physics-based and data-driven simulation** for **performance analysis** and **optimization**.
    - **Technologies**:
      - **Building Energy Simulation Tools**: **EnergyPlus**, **ESP-r**, or **OpenStudio** for **thermal/energy modeling**.
      - **Structural Analysis Tools**: **ANSYS**, **SAP2000**, or **OpenSees** for **dynamic structural behavior** under operational loads.
      - **Digital Twin Platforms**: **Custom or proprietary platforms** (e.g., **Siemens Digital Twin**, **Autodesk Twin**) with **co-simulation capabilities** for **multi-domain analysis**.
  - **Data Management**:
    - **Role**: **Real-time data ingestion**, **storage**, and **processing** for **dynamic twin synchronization**.
    - **Technologies**:
      - **Time-Series Databases**: **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**, or **Apache Kafka** for **high-velocity IoT data** handling.
      - **Graph Databases**: **Neo4j** or **ArangoDB** for **relationship-aware infrastructure modeling** (e.g., **system dependencies**, **spatial hierarchies**).
      - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid Architectures**: **AWS IoT Core**, **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**, or **on-premise edge nodes** for **low-latency processing**.
  - **AI/ML Enablers**:
    - **Role**: **Predictive and prescriptive analytics** for **autonomous decision-making**.
    - **Technologies**:
      - **Supervised Learning**: **Random Forests**, **Gradient Boosting** for **failure prediction** and **energy forecasting**.
      - **Reinforcement Learning**: **Q-Learning**, **Deep RL** for **adaptive control policies** (e.g., HVAC optimization).
      - **Digital Twin-Specific ML**: **Federated learning** for **privacy-preserving cross-site analytics**, **transfer learning** for **domain adaptation** across projects.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Role**: **Model authoring**, **twin lifecycle management**, and **collaborative development**.
    - **Technologies**:
      - **BIM Authoring Tools**: **Revit**, **ArchiCAD**, **Bentley MicroStation** for **static model creation**.
      - **Digital Twin SDKs**: **Custom APIs** or **platform-specific SDKs** (e.g., **NVIDIA Omniverse**, **Unity for DT**) for **twin customization**.
      - **Version Control**: **GitLFS**, **Perforce** for **large-model collaboration**.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Digital Twin Middleware**: **Middleware layers** (e.g., **OSIsoft PI System**, **PTC ThingWorx**) for **data normalization** and **service orchestration**.
    - **Digital Thread**: **End-to-end data lineage** tools (e.g., **Siemens Teamcenter**, **Dassault 3DEXPERIENCE**) to **trace decisions** from design to operation.
    - **Security and Compliance**: **Blockchain** (e.g., **Hyperledger Fabric**) for **immutable audit trails**, **zero-trust architectures** for **cyber-physical security**.
  The tooling stack emphasizes **interoperability**, **scalability**, and **domain-specific adaptability** to address the **fragmented nature of AECO data** and **real-time operational demands**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin (DT) system in the AECO domain may involve **multiple digital twin instances** to address the **complex and heterogeneous infrastructure environments**, where each instance could represent distinct assets (e.g., individual buildings, subsystems, or components) within a larger system. The architecture may adopt **decentralized or hybrid approaches** to manage multiplicities, ensuring **coordination mechanisms** are in place to synchronize data and models across instances. The **scope of responsibilities** for each twin instance is likely defined by its specific domain (e.g., structural, mechanical, or operational systems), with **hierarchical dependencies** ensuring alignment with overarching system goals. However, the document does not specify exact multiplicities (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, or N:M mappings) or centralized/decentralized deployment strategies beyond the need for systematic and efficient deployment in fragmented environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin constellation** in the AECO domain requires **orchestration and coordination mechanisms** to integrate **static models** (e.g., BIM, CAD) with **runtime data** (e.g., IoT sensors, operational logs) into a unified cyber-physical system. The architecture must support **component integration** across heterogeneous data sources and models, leveraging **interoperability standards** (e.g., IFC, CityGML, or domain-specific ontologies) to ensure seamless data exchange. **Resource management** is critical, particularly in large-scale deployments, where computational and storage demands must be balanced with real-time performance requirements. The system-wide coordination relies on **predictive analytics and simulation engines** to enable data-driven decision-making, though the document does not specify exact orchestration frameworks (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) or real-time synchronization protocols (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system in the AECO domain must support **horizontal integration** with **external systems** through standardized **data exchange protocols** and **interoperability frameworks** to bridge fragmented models and data silos. Key dependencies include integration with **enterprise systems** (e.g., ERP, CMMS), **domain-specific tools** (e.g., BIM platforms like Revit or ArchiCAD), and **IoT/OT ecosystems** (e.g., sensor networks, PLCs). The document emphasizes the need for **cross-domain collaboration** and **real-time data synchronization**, though it does not specify exact protocols (e.g., REST APIs, GraphQL, or proprietary interfaces) or integration patterns (e.g., API gateways, middleware layers). The system’s effectiveness hinges on adhering to **industry standards** (e.g., ISO 19650, BIM Collaboration Format) to ensure compatibility with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as a foundational semantic layer, addressing its limitations in representing **Building Control Systems (BCS)** components. The framework supports **geometric models** (inherited from BIM) and **behavioral models** (enriched with device-specific configurations such as sensors, actuators, and controllers) to create a unified semantic foundation. **Analytical models** are derived through **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles, enabling automated extraction of structured representations from BIM and generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Data components include **real-time operational data** (e.g., sensor readings, actuator states) and **static metadata** (e.g., device specifications, control logic). The framework ensures **model relationships** are maintained via semantic mappings between BIM elements and DT components, while **data management** is handled through automated synchronization pipelines between physical and digital domains. The modular architecture supports **custom services and plugins**, allowing dynamic extension of DT functionality with domain-specific analytics or simulations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The DT system incorporates **sensors** as part of the **Building Control Systems (BCS)**, which are modeled and configured by **Building Control Engineers** within the BIM environment. While specific sensor types (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption) are not detailed, the framework supports their integration to enable **real-time data exchange** between physical infrastructure and the DT. The sensing infrastructure is designed to provide **continuous monitoring** of building operations, with data transmitted to the DT for synchronization. The document does not specify **sampling frequencies, spatial distribution, accuracy/precision, or transmission protocols**, but the system relies on these components to feed **operational state data** into the DT for analysis and control.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework addresses **model fidelity** by bridging the gap between BIM’s **static representations** and the DT’s **dynamic operational requirements**, ensuring that enriched BIM models (with device-specific configurations) are accurately transformed into deployable DT artifacts. **Validation and verification** are implicit in the **automated generation process**, where structured representations are derived from BIM with minimal manual intervention, reducing human error. **Uncertainty sources** may arise from discrepancies between BIM models and real-world BCS components, though the document does not specify quantification methods. **Error detection and correction** are supported through **real-time data exchange**, enabling continuous synchronization between physical and digital systems. **Quality assurance** is embedded in the **modular architecture**, where standardized extension points allow for integration of custom validation services or analytics to monitor DT accuracy and operational integrity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical_to_virtual_interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by **real-time bidirectional data exchange** between the physical building infrastructure and its digital counterpart. The data flows include **sensor telemetry** (e.g., environmental metrics like temperature, humidity, occupancy, and equipment status) and **BIM-enriched model data** (e.g., geometric, spatial, and semantic building information). Transmission occurs via **standardized communication protocols** (not explicitly specified but implied to support interoperability with heterogeneous systems). The system employs **event-triggered data transmission** (e.g., threshold breaches, scheduled intervals, or dynamic operational changes) to ensure synchronization. **Data quality assurance** is implicitly addressed through the framework’s **modular architecture**, which allows for preprocessing steps like validation, normalization, and filtering before integration. The **data integration mechanisms** leverage the DT Framework’s central hub to consolidate disparate data streams into a unified operational model, enabling seamless interaction between Building Control Engineers (who deploy device models) and Facility Managers (who monitor in real-time).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual_to_physical_interaction** in this DT system involves **control commands** generated by the digital twin and transmitted to physical building systems for execution. These commands include **actuation signals** (e.g., HVAC adjustments, lighting control, or security system triggers) and **configuration updates** (e.g., runtime adjustments to operational parameters). The **control message formats** are standardized to ensure compatibility with integrated services, though specific formats (e.g., JSON, MQTT payloads) are not detailed. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are implied to enforce security and prevent unauthorized modifications, aligning with the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** for interoperability and maintainability. **Feedback systems** provide confirmation of command execution (e.g., acknowledgment signals or status updates from physical devices), while **conflict resolution** is managed through the DT Framework’s **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments without full redeployment. The system ensures **command execution confirmation** via bidirectional data flows, closing the loop between digital control and physical response.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **dt_technical_connection** infrastructure is designed to support **heterogeneous system interoperability** and **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital components. The system employs a **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points**, enabling integration across diverse protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or proprietary IoT standards). The **network topology** is centralized, with the DT Framework acting as a **hub-and-spoke model** to facilitate bidirectional data exchange. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are optimized for low-overhead updates and real-time monitoring, though exact metrics (e.g., <100ms latency) are not specified. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are implied to address scalability and maintainability, ensuring continuous operation even during component failures. **Security protocols** for network communication are not explicitly detailed but are inferred to align with the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF_X)**, which emphasize accessibility and interoperability while mitigating risks in heterogeneous environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **dt_hosting_deployment** infrastructure supports **multi-platform deployment models**, including **cloud, edge, and on-premise** configurations, to accommodate varying operational needs. The system’s **computational and storage requirements** are dynamically scalable, enabling adjustments from individual components to entire infrastructure systems without full redeployment. The **deployment model** leverages the DT Framework’s **runtime configuration management**, allowing for modular service integration and low-overhead updates. **Availability and reliability** are assured through the framework’s **modular architecture** and **non-functional requirements (NF_X)**, which prioritize maintainability and fault tolerance. The deployment process begins with **Building Control Engineers** using BIM tools to model devices and initiate deployment, followed by **Facility Managers** interacting with the operational DT for real-time monitoring. The system’s **scalability mechanisms** ensure seamless expansion as new services or analytics are integrated by **DT Engineers**, maintaining operational continuity throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the proposed model-driven deployment framework spans from **initial device modeling** to **runtime operation**, addressing the gap between static Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations and dynamic DT requirements. Key phases include:
  1. **Device Modeling**: Building Control Engineers define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment, enriching static BIM models with **semantic representations** of Building Control System (BCS) components (e.g., functional requirements **F_X** in Table III). This phase establishes a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations.
  2. **Model Transformation**: Leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, the framework extracts **structured representations** from enriched BIM models and **automatically generates deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., executable models, simulation environments) with **minimal manual intervention**.
  3. **DT Deployment**: The generated artifacts are deployed to synchronize the physical building and its digital twin, supported by **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous bidirectional synchronization.
  4. **Runtime Operation**: The DT operates in tandem with the physical infrastructure, enabling **interactive visualization** (immersive interfaces) and **modular service integration** (e.g., custom analytics, control logic extensions). Facility Managers interact with the twin for **monitoring and operational control**, while DT Engineers extend functionality via **custom services and plugins**.
  **Transition Management**: The framework ensures seamless transitions between phases through **standardized extension points** and **modular architecture**, allowing dynamic adaptation to evolving operational requirements. **Lifecycle governance** is implicit in the framework’s design, addressing theoretical and practical insights from research projects like **TwinLight and BOREALIS** (Section II).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution in this framework are governed by a **model-driven approach** integrating **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** and **automated artifact generation**. Key aspects include:
  - **Engineering Methodologies**: The process begins with **device modeling** in BIM, where Building Control Engineers define **BCS components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) and **control logic**. This is followed by **MDE-based transformation** to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts (e.g., simulation models, runtime environments) with **minimal manual intervention**.
  - **Quality Assurance**: Implicit in the framework’s **automated generation** of DT artifacts from semantically enriched BIM models, ensuring consistency between physical and digital representations. The **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points** supports **plug-and-play integration** of validated services.
  - **Testing and Validation**: The framework’s **real-time data exchange** capabilities enable continuous synchronization, validating the DT’s accuracy against physical system states. **Interactive visualization** provides immersive interfaces for **runtime monitoring and validation** by Facility Managers.
  - **Version Control**: Not explicitly detailed in the document, but the **automated generation** of artifacts from BIM suggests versioning may be tied to **BIM model updates** or **DT service revisions**, with potential integration of **versioned MDE transformations**.
  - **Evolution Management**: The DT’s modular design allows **runtime extension** via **custom services and plugins** developed by DT Engineers. Evolution is managed through **standardized extension points**, enabling incremental updates without disrupting core functionality. The framework’s alignment with **TwinLight and BOREALIS** projects implies iterative refinement based on **operational feedback and research insights**.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision-making capabilities** through **real-time data exchange**, **interactive visualization**, and **modular analytics integration**. Key components include:
  - **Types of Insights**: Generated from **continuous synchronization** between physical and digital twins, insights encompass **system state monitoring** (e.g., sensor/actuator performance), **control logic validation**, and **predictive operational analytics** (e.g., energy efficiency, fault detection).
  - **Analytics and Processing**: The DT leverages **custom services and plugins** (developed by DT Engineers) for advanced analytics, such as **real-time anomaly detection**, **optimization algorithms**, or **machine learning-based predictions**. The **modular architecture** allows integration of third-party tools (e.g., simulation engines, AI models).
  - **Decision-Making Processes**: Facility Managers interact with the twin via **immersive interfaces** to **monitor system states**, **adjust control logic**, or **initiate corrective actions**. Decisions are supported by **visualized insights** (e.g., dashboards, 3D models) and **automated recommendations** from integrated services.
  - **User Interaction Methods**: Primarily **graphical interfaces** (e.g., BIM-based visualizations) and **runtime control panels** for Facility Managers. Building Control Engineers interact during **device modeling** and **logic definition**, while DT Engineers extend functionality via **APIs or plugin frameworks**.
  - **Confidence Measures**: Not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **real-time synchronization** and **automated validation** imply high confidence in insights derived from **direct physical-digital correlation**. Custom services may incorporate **uncertainty quantification** (e.g., probabilistic models) if integrated.

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
